description: The Questionnaire for User Interaction Satisfaction (QUIS) is a measurement tool designed to assess a computer user's subjective satisfaction with the human-computer interface. The QUIS contains measures of overall system satisfaction and specific interface factors such as screen visibility, terminology and system information, learning factors, and system capabilities. This library includes six components, one for each individual dimension, as well as a sequence containing all dimensions.
reference:
Chin, J. P., Diehl, V. A. and Norman, K. L. (1988). Development of an instrument measuring user satisfaction of the human-computer interface. Proceedings of SIGCHI '88, (pp. 213-218), New York: ACM/SIGCHI. 

 Chin, J. P., Norman, K. L., and Shneiderman, B. (1987). Subjective user evaluation of CF PASCAL programming tools.  Technical Report (CAR-TR-304).  College Park, MD:  Human-Computer Interaction Laboratory, Center for Automation Research, University of Maryland. 

 Harper, B. D. and Norman, K. L. (1993). Improving User Satisfaction: The Questionnaire for User Interaction Satisfaction Version 5.5. Proceedings of the 1st Annual Mid-Atlantic Human Factors Conference, (pp. 224-228), Virginia Beach, VA.

questionOptions.label: 0: Terrible, 9: Wonderful
0: Difficult, 9: Easy
0: Frustrating, 9: Satisfying
0: Inadequate power, 9: Adequate power
0: Dull, 9: Stimulating
0: Rigid, 9: Flexible

Characters on the computer screen

(0: Not at all, 9: Very much)

(0: Confusing, 9: Very clear)

(0: Confusing, 9: Very clear)

Use of terms throughout system  
(0: Inconsistent, 9: Consistent)

Computer terminology is related to the task you are doing  
(0: Never, 9: Always)

Position of messages on screen  
(0: Inconsistent, 9: Consistent)

Messages on screen which prompt user for input  
(0: Confusing, 9: Clear)

Computer keeps you informed about what it is doing  
(0: Never, 9: Always)

Error messages  
(0: Unhelpful, 9: Helpful)

Learning to operate the system  
(0: Difficult, 9: Easy)

Remembering names and use of commands  
(0: Difficult, 9: Easy)

(0: Never, 9: Always)

(0: Unhelpful, 9: Helpful)

(0: Confusing, 9: Clear)

(0: Too slow, 9: Fast enough)

(0: Unreliable, 9: Reliable)

(0: Noisy, 9: Quiet)

(0: Difficult, 9: Easy)

Experienced and inexperienced users' needs are taken into consideration  
(0: Never, 9: Always)

(0: Poor, 9: Good)

(0: Poor, 9: Good)

(0: Awkward, 9: Gracious)

System clutter and UI "noise"  
(0: Poor, 9: Good)
